examples.title: Contiguous File Allocation
examples.description: Demonstrates contiguous allocation where files are assigned consecutive blocks
examples.code:
PROCEDURE AllocateFileContiguous(filename, fileSize)
  blocksNeeded = CEILING(fileSize / blockSize)
  
  // Find contiguous free space
  startBlock = FindContiguousFreeBlocks(blocksNeeded)
  
  IF startBlock == -1 THEN
    RETURN Error: "Insufficient contiguous space"
  END IF
  
  // Allocate blocks
  FOR i FROM 0 TO blocksNeeded - 1 DO
    AllocateBlock(startBlock + i)
  END FOR
  
  // Update file allocation table
  FAT[filename].startBlock = startBlock
  FAT[filename].length = blocksNeeded
  
  RETURN Success
END PROCEDURE

PROCEDURE ReadFileContiguous(filename, position, bytes)
  blockNumber = position / blockSize
  offset = position % blockSize
  
  startBlock = FAT[filename].startBlock
  targetBlock = startBlock + blockNumber
  
  READ_BLOCK(targetBlock, buffer, offset, bytes)
  RETURN buffer
END PROCEDURE
examples.explanation: Contiguous allocation stores files in consecutive disk blocks. The algorithm searches for a contiguous range of free blocks of required size, then marks them as allocated in the FAT. Reading requires calculating the block number from the start position and reading directly - O(1) random access.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for allocation, O(1) for access

examples.title: Linked File Allocation
examples.description: Demonstrates linked allocation using pointer-based block chaining
examples.code:
PROCEDURE AllocateFileLinked(filename, fileSize)
  blocksNeeded = CEILING(fileSize / blockSize)
  previousBlock = -1
  firstBlock = -1
  
  FOR i FROM 1 TO blocksNeeded DO
    // Find any free block
    freeBlock = FindFreeBlock()
    
    IF freeBlock == -1 THEN
      RETURN Error: "No free blocks available"
    END IF
    
    // Link to previous block
    IF previousBlock != -1 THEN
      SetBlockPointer(previousBlock, freeBlock)
    ELSE
      firstBlock = freeBlock  // Save first block
    END IF
    
    // Mark block as allocated and clear next pointer
    AllocateBlock(freeBlock)
    SetBlockPointer(fileBlock, -1)  // -1 indicates end of chain
    previousBlock = freeBlock
  END FOR
  
  // Store file entry
  Directory[filename].firstBlock = firstBlock
  Directory[filename].blocks = blocksNeeded
  
  RETURN Success
END PROCEDURE

PROCEDURE ReadFileLinked(filename, position)
  blockOffset = position / blockSize
  currentBlock = Directory[filename].firstBlock
  
  // Traverse to target block
  FOR i FROM 0 TO blockOffset - 1 DO
    currentBlock = GetBlockPointer(currentBlock)
    IF currentBlock == -1 THEN
      RETURN Error: "Position beyond file"
    END IF
  END IF
  
  RETURN ReadBlock(currentBlock)
END PROCEDURE
examples.explanation: Linked allocation stores files as linked lists of blocks. Each block contains a pointer to the next block. This eliminates external fragmentation but does not support efficient random access - must traverse from the beginning (O(n) access time).
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for allocation and random access
examples.spaceComplexity: O(1) - pointer stored in each block

examples.title: Indexed File Allocation
examples.description: Demonstrates indexed allocation with index blocks pointing to data blocks
examples.code:
PROCEDURE AllocateFileIndexed(filename, fileSize)
  blocksNeeded = CEILING(fileSize / blockSize)
  
  // Allocate index block
  indexBlock = FindFreeBlock()
  IF indexBlock == -1 THEN
    RETURN Error: "No free blocks"
  END IF
  
  AllocateBlock(indexBlock)
  
  // Allocate data blocks and update index
  FOR i FROM 0 TO blocksNeeded - 1 DO
    dataBlock = FindFreeBlock()
    
    IF dataBlock == -1 THEN
      RETURN Error: "Insufficient space"
    END IF
    
    AllocateBlock(dataBlock)
    SetIndexEntry(indexBlock, i, dataBlock)  // Index block[i] = dataBlock
  END FOR
  
  // Store file entry pointing to index block
  Directory[filename].indexBlock = indexBlock
  Directory[filename].size = fileSize
  
  RETURN Success
END PROCEDURE

PROCEDURE ReadFileIndexed(filename, position)
  blockNumber = position / blockSize
  indexBlock = Directory[filename].indexBlock
  
  // Direct access through index
  dataBlock = GetIndexEntry(indexBlock, blockNumber)
  
  IF dataBlock == -1 THEN
    RETURN Error: "Invalid position"
  END IF
  
  offset = position % blockSize
  RETURN ReadBlockAtOffset(dataBlock, offset)
END PROCEDURE

// For large files - multi-level index
PROCEDURE ReadFileDoubleIndexed(filename, position)
  blockNumber = position / blockSize
  indexBlock = Directory[filename].indexBlock
  
  IF blockNumber < 12 THEN  // Direct blocks
    dataBlock = GetIndexEntry(indexBlock, blockNumber)
  ELSE IF blockNumber < 268 THEN  // Single indirect
    singleIndirect = GetIndexEntry(indexBlock, 12)
    dataBlock = GetIndexEntry(singleIndirect, blockNumber - 12)
  ELSE  // Double indirect
    doubleIndirect = GetIndexEntry(indexBlock, 13)
    offset = blockNumber - 268
    singleIndirect = GetIndexEntry(doubleIndirect, offset / 256)
    dataBlock = GetIndexEntry(singleIndirect, offset % 256)
  END IF
  
  RETURN ReadBlock(dataBlock)
END PROCEDURE
examples.explanation: Indexed allocation uses an index block to store pointers to all data blocks. It supports efficient random access (O(1)) without external fragmentation. UNIX uses a hybrid approach with direct, single indirect, and double indirect pointers to handle files of different sizes efficiently.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for direct access, O(1) for indexed (constant disk seeks)
examples.spaceComplexity: O(1) index block per file plus pointer overhead in blocks

examples.title: Free Space Management - Bit Vector
examples.description: Demonstrates free space management using bit vector/bitmap
examples.code:
// Global bit vector - one bit per block
// 0 = free, 1 = allocated
bitVector = ARRAY[totalBlocks] OF {0}

PROCEDURE InitializeBitVector()
  FOR i FROM 0 TO totalBlocks - 1 DO
    bitVector[i] = 0  // All blocks initially free
  END FOR
  bitVector[0] = 1  // Mark boot block as allocated
  bitVector[1] = 1  // Mark superblock as allocated
  // Mark inode table and other reserved blocks as allocated
END PROCEDURE

PROCEDURE AllocateBlock()
  FOR i FROM 0 TO totalBlocks - 1 DO
    IF bitVector[i] == 0 THEN
      bitVector[i] = 1  // Mark as allocated
      RETURN i  // Return block number
    END IF
  END FOR
  RETURN -1  // No free block
END PROCEDURE

PROCEDURE FreeBlock(blockNumber)
  IF blockNumber >= 0 AND blockNumber < totalBlocks THEN
    bitVector[blockNumber] = 0
    RETURN Success
  ELSE
    RETURN Error: "Invalid block number"
  END IF
END PROCEDURE

PROCEDURE FindContiguousFreeBlocks(count)
  consecutive = 0
  startBlock = -1
  
  FOR i FROM 0 TO totalBlocks - 1 DO
    IF bitVector[i] == 0 THEN
      IF consecutive == 0 THEN
        startBlock = i
      END IF
      consecutive = consecutive + 1
      
      IF consecutive >= count THEN
        RETURN startBlock  // Found contiguous space
      END IF
    ELSE
      consecutive = 0
      startBlock = -1
    END IF
  END FOR
  
  RETURN -1  // Not enough contiguous space
END PROCEDURE

PROCEDURE GetFreeBlockCount()
  freeCount = 0
  FOR i FROM 0 TO totalBlocks - 1 DO
    IF bitVector[i] == 0 THEN
      freeCount = freeCount + 1
    END IF
  END FOR
  RETURN freeCount
END PROCEDURE
examples.explanation: The bit vector (bitmap) method uses one bit per disk block to track allocation status. Allocation searches for a 0 bit, setting it to 1 when allocated. Freeing a block resets the bit to 0. Efficient for finding contiguous blocks but requires memory proportional to disk size (e.g., 2.5MB for 1TB disk with 4KB blocks).
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) worst case for allocation/freeing, O(n) for finding contiguous blocks
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) bits where n = number of blocks (n/8 bytes)
